title: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease

items.subtopic: Bartonella Infections, Including Cat-Scratch Disease | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding the Etiology and Clinical Manifestations of Bartonella Infections
items.content: Bartonella infections are caused by a genus of fastidious, facultative intracellular bacteria that have adapted to specific mammalian hosts. These species can evade the immune system through modification of virulence factors and chronic bacteremia in their animal reservoirs. Human infection is often incidental, except for B. quintana and B. bacilliformis. Clinical presentations vary based on the infecting Bartonella species and the host's immune status.
keyPoints: Bartonella species are fastidious, facultative intracellular bacteria with over 40 distinct species or subspecies; at least 20 recognized as human pathogens
Adaptation to specific mammals creates a reservoir for human infections through chronic bacteremia
Clinical presentation depends on the infecting Bartonella species and host immune status
B. henselae is the principal agent of cat-scratch disease (CSD)
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · Bartonella Infections, Including Cat-Scratch Disease | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Bartonella Infections, Including Cat-Scratch Disease | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Challenges in Treating Bartonella Infections and Clinical Presentation Variability
items.content: Although Bartonella species are susceptible to many antibiotics in vitro, clinical responses often do not correlate with minimal inhibitory concentrations observed in laboratory settings. This discrepancy highlights the challenge of treating these infections effectively in vivo. Cat-scratch disease (CSD) typically presents as a self-limited illness with two main forms: typical CSD characterized by subacute regional lymphadenopathy and atypical or extranodal CSD involving various organs.
keyPoints: Bartonella species show poor correlation between in vitro antibiotic susceptibility and in vivo efficacy
Cat-scratch disease (CSD) is usually self-limited with two clinical presentations: typical (subacute regional lymphadenopathy) and atypical (extranodal manifestations)
B. henselae is the main etiologic agent of CSD, though rare cases involve Afipia felis and other Bartonella species
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · Bartonella Infections, Including Cat-Scratch Disease | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS
items.title: Epidemiology and Transmission of Cat Scratch Disease (CSD)
items.content: Cat Scratch Disease occurs worldwide, with higher incidence in warm climates. It peaks during fall and winter in temperate regions. Cats, particularly kittens, are the primary reservoirs, while cat fleas facilitate transmission among cats. Human infection typically follows contact with infected cats or through scratches/bites.
keyPoints: CSD occurs worldwide, favoring warm and humid climates.
Incidence peaks during fall and winter in temperate climates.
Estimated annual disease incidence in the U.S. is 4–9 outpatient cases per 100,000 population.
Higher incidence among those living in southern United States and children aged 5–9 years.
About 5–10% of patients are hospitalized in the U.S.; higher hospitalization rates reported in Israel.
Cats, especially kittens, are major reservoirs; cat fleas facilitate transmission.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS
items.title: Pathogenesis of Cat Scratch Disease (CSD)
items.content: The pathogenesis involves B. henselae transmission from cats to humans, likely through saliva on claws or flea feces inoculated by scratches/bites. In immunocompetent hosts, a TH1 response leads to necrotizing granulomatous lymphadenitis.
keyPoints: Mode of cat-to-human transmission involves B. henselae in cat saliva and flea feces.
Infection can occur via scratches, bites, or mucous membrane contact.
TH1 response results in necrotizing granulomatous lymphadenitis.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS
items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Prognosis of Cat Scratch Disease (CSD)
items.content: Typical CSD presents with a primary lesion at the inoculation site, followed by lymphadenopathy. Symptoms include fever, malaise, and anorexia. Atypical presentations can mimic serious conditions like lymphoma or malignancy. Resolution is slow but usually complete without treatment.
keyPoints: 85–90% of patients have typical CSD with primary lesion and lymphadenopathy.
Lymphadenopathy develops 1–3 weeks after cat contact, often painful and sometimes suppurative (~10%).
Common symptoms: fever (50%), malaise, anorexia; low-grade fever may rise to ≥39°C.
Atypical CSD occurs in 10–15% of patients, with manifestations like Parinaud's syndrome, neuroretinitis, and FUO.
Resolution can take weeks to months for symptoms, longer for node shrinkage.
Typical/atypical CSD usually resolves without treatment; some ophthalmologic issues may cause vision loss.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Cat-Scratch Disease
items.title: When to Consider PCR for Definitive Diagnosis in Seronegative CSD Patients
items.content: PCR of pus aspirated from lymph nodes or the primary inoculation lesion is highly sensitive and specific, making it particularly useful for definitive and rapid diagnosis in seronegative patients. This approach helps overcome challenges with variable sensitivity and specificity associated with serologic testing.
keyPoints: Serologic tests have variable sensitivity and specificity; IgG presence without IgM may complicate interpretation.
PCR of pus aspirated from lymph nodes or primary lesion is highly sensitive and specific for CSD diagnosis in seronegative patients.
Consider PCR when serologic results are inconclusive, especially in the absence of seroconversion.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · DIAGNOSIS | Cat-Scratch Disease

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Cat-Scratch Disease
items.title: Indications for Lymph Node Biopsy in Suspected CSD
items.content: In cases where clinical data are less supportive of CSD, or when seronegative patients present with severe complications like encephalitis or neuroretinitis, lymph node biopsy is preferred over fine-needle aspiration. This approach helps establish a specific diagnosis and rule out other conditions.
keyPoints: Lymph node biopsy is preferred over fine-needle aspiration in less supportive CSD cases or seronegative patients with severe complications.
Consider malignancy when acute- and convalescent-phase sera are negative (10–20% of CSD patients), lymphadenopathy does not regress, or constitutional symptoms persist.
Differential diagnosis includes pyogenic lymphadenitis, mycobacterial infection, brucellosis, syphilis, tularemia, plague, toxoplasmosis, sporotrichosis, and histoplasmosis.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · DIAGNOSIS | Cat-Scratch Disease

items.subtopic: Cat-Scratch Disease | PREVENTION | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Prevention of Cat-Scratch Disease in High-Risk Patients
items.content: For immunocompromised patients and those with valvular heart disease, avoiding cats (especially kittens) and implementing flea control measures are recommended to prevent cat-scratch disease.
keyPoints: Avoiding contact with cats, particularly kittens, is advised for high-risk groups.
Flea control is a preventive measure for immunocompromised patients and those with valvular heart disease.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · Cat-Scratch Disease | PREVENTION | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cat-Scratch Disease | PREVENTION | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Treatment Approach for Cat-Scratch Disease in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: In immunocompromised patients, systemic antibiotics are recommended to manage cat-scratch disease. Suppurative nodes should be drained using large-bore needle aspiration rather than incision and drainage.
keyPoints: Systemic antibiotics are advised for immunocompromised individuals with cat-scratch disease.
Suppurative nodes should be managed by large-bore needle aspiration to prevent chronic draining tracts.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · Cat-Scratch Disease | PREVENTION | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | B. quintana Bacteremia | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Clinical Spectrum and Incubation of B. quintana Infections
items.content: B. quintana infections present with a range of clinical manifestations from mild febrile illness to severe conditions like endocarditis. The typical incubation period is 15–25 days, but can vary from 3 to 38 days. Symptoms include periodic fever lasting 4–5 days with intervals of 3-8 days, headache, back and limb pain, profuse sweating, shivering, myalgia, arthralgia, splenomegaly, maculopapular rash, and nuchal rigidity.
keyPoints: Typical incubation period: 15–25 days (range, 3–38 days)
Fever is periodic, lasting 4–5 days with intervals of 3-8 days
Symptoms include headache, back and limb pain, profuse sweating, shivering, myalgia, arthralgia, splenomegaly, maculopapular rash (occasional), nuchal rigidity (some cases)
Untreated disease duration: 4–6 weeks
Death is rare
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | B. quintana Bacteremia | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | B. quintana Bacteremia | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of B. quintana Bacteremia
items.content: Definitive diagnosis of B. quintana bacteremia requires isolation by blood culture, which can remain positive for weeks. Acute trench fever patients develop significant titers of Bartonella antibodies; chronic cases may be seronegative. High IgG antibody titers necessitate evaluation for endocarditis. Treatment with gentamicin and doxycycline is superior to placebo in eradicating bacteremia, crucial even in mild cases to prevent endocarditis.
keyPoints: Definitive diagnosis: isolation of B. quintana by blood culture
Positive blood cultures can persist for weeks
Acute trench fever: significant antibody titers; chronic cases may be seronegative
High IgG antibodies warrant endocarditis evaluation
Treatment with gentamicin and doxycycline is superior to placebo in eradicating bacteremia
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | B. quintana Bacteremia | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Bartonella Endocarditis
items.title: Risk Factors and Valve Involvement in Bartonella Endocarditis
items.content: Bartonella endocarditis is more common in adults, particularly males. B. quintana is linked to homelessness, alcoholism, and louse infestation, while B. henselae is associated with cat exposure. Most cases involve native valves, especially the aortic valve (~60%). Patients with B. henselae often have preexisting valvulopathy, whereas B. quintana can infect normal valves.
keyPoints: Bartonella endocarditis occurs worldwide, more in adults and males.
Risk factors for B. quintana include homelessness, alcoholism, louse infestation; no risk factors needed for diagnosis.
B. henselae linked to cat exposure.
~60% of cases involve the aortic valve.
B. henselae often infects preexisting valvulopathy; B. quintana can infect normal valves.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Bartonella Endocarditis

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Bartonella Endocarditis
items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Treatment of Bartonella Endocarditis
items.content: Diagnosing Bartonella endocarditis is challenging due to low positive blood culture rates (~25%) and cross-reactivity in serologic tests. High-titer IgG antibodies (≥1:800) are indicative, but PCR and DNA sequencing on valve tissue are definitive for species identification. Empirical treatment includes gentamicin, doxycycline, and ceftriaxone; aminoglycosides should be used for ≥2 weeks once diagnosis is confirmed.
keyPoints: Blood cultures positive in ~25% of cases, mainly B. quintana.
Serologic tests show high-titer IgG antibodies (≥1:800).
Cross-reactivity with C. burnetii and Chlamydia species.
PCR and DNA sequencing on valve tissue confirm diagnosis.
Empirical treatment: gentamicin, doxycycline, ceftriaxone; aminoglycosides for ≥2 weeks post-diagnosis.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Bartonella Endocarditis

items.subtopic: DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis: Clinical Presentation and Etiology
items.content: Bacillary angiomatosis is an angioproliferative disorder caused by B. henselae or B. quintana, leading to neovascular proliferative lesions in severely immunocompromised patients. It presents as cutaneous lesions and can involve the liver, spleen, and bones. Bacillary peliosis, primarily involving the liver, is characterized by blood-filled cystic structures.
keyPoints: Caused by B. henselae or B. quintana
Characterized by neovascular proliferative lesions
Cutaneous lesions may be tan, red, or purple
Hepatosplenic lesions caused only by B. henselae
Subcutaneous and lytic bone lesions more frequent with B. quintana
Bacillary peliosis involves liver (peliosis hepatitis), spleen, lymph nodes
Cystic structures range from microscopic to several millimeters
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Bartonella Infections
items.content: Bacillary angiomatosis and peliosis primarily affect HIV-infected individuals with CD4+ T-cell counts <100/μL. The incidence has decreased due to antiretroviral therapy and prophylactic antibiotics. Contact with cats or cat fleas increases B. henselae risk, while low income, homelessness, and body louse infestation are risk factors for B. quintana.
keyPoints: Primarily affects HIV-infected persons with CD4+ T-cell counts <100/μL
Incidence decreased with antiretroviral therapy
Contact with cats or cat fleas increases B. henselae risk
Risk factors for B. quintana: low income, homelessness, body louse infestation
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS | Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.title: Histologic Diagnosis of Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis
items.content: Bacillary angiomatosis is characterized by lobular proliferation of small blood vessels with enlarged endothelial cells, mixed infiltrates predominantly neutrophils, and Warthin-Starry silver staining revealing clusters of bacilli. Peliosis presents as cystic lesions partially lined by endothelial cells surrounded by fibromyxoid stroma.
keyPoints: Histologic examination reveals lobular proliferation of small blood vessels with enlarged endothelial cells in bacillary angiomatosis.
Peliotic lesions are small, blood-filled cysts several millimeters in size, partially lined by endothelial cells.
Warthin-Starry silver staining shows clusters of bacilli in both conditions.
Blood cultures are usually negative.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · PATHOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS | Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS | Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.title: Treatment Recommendations for Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis
items.content: Both bacillary angiomatosis and peliosis should be treated with prolonged therapy using a macrolide or doxycycline.
keyPoints: Prolonged therapy with a macrolide or doxycycline is recommended for both conditions.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · PATHOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS | Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: CSD-associated fever of unknown origin (FUO) may mimic malignancy.
items.explanation: CSD-associated FUO is a unique syndrome that often mimics malignancy.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Cat-Scratch Disease
items.statement: Histopathology findings in CSD are specific and definitive for diagnosis.
items.explanation: Histopathology findings, such as stellate granulomata with necrosis, are nonspecific.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · DIAGNOSIS | Cat-Scratch Disease

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Cat-Scratch Disease
items.statement: PCR of a lymph node biopsy specimen is less sensitive due to potential sampling error.
items.explanation: PCR of a lymph node biopsy may be less sensitive, potentially because of sampling error.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · DIAGNOSIS | Cat-Scratch Disease

items.subtopic: Cat-Scratch Disease | PREVENTION | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: Trench fever is caused by B. quintana and was first described in World War I.
items.explanation: Trench fever, also known as 5-day fever or quintan fever, is caused by B. quintana and was first described during World War I.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · Cat-Scratch Disease | PREVENTION | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cat-Scratch Disease | PREVENTION | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: Macaque monkeys are a known reservoir for human infections of B. quintana.
items.explanation: Macaque monkeys are natural hosts of B. quintana, but there is no evidence for macaque-to-human transmission.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · Cat-Scratch Disease | PREVENTION | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | B. quintana Bacteremia | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.statement: B. quintana can cause culture-negative endocarditis.
items.explanation: Bartonella species, including B. quintana, are common pathogens of culture-negative endocarditis.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | B. quintana Bacteremia | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | B. quintana Bacteremia | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Patients with chronic B. quintana bacteremia always have significant titers of antibody to Bartonella.
items.explanation: Patients with chronic B. quintana bacteremia may be seronegative.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | B. quintana Bacteremia | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Bartonella Endocarditis
items.statement: Blood cultures are positive in more than 50% of cases for both B. quintana and B. henselae endocarditis.
items.explanation: Blood cultures are positive in only ~25% of cases, mostly those caused by B. quintana.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Bartonella Endocarditis

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Bartonella Endocarditis
items.statement: Approximately 60% of patients with Bartonella endocarditis undergo valvular surgery.
items.explanation: The proportion of patients who undergo surgery is high, at ~60%, likely due to delayed diagnosis.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Bartonella Endocarditis

items.subtopic: DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Bacillary angiomatosis is primarily seen in immunocompetent patients.
items.explanation: Bacillary angiomatosis occurs primarily in HIV-infected persons with CD4+ T-cell counts of <100/μL and other immunosuppressed patients.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Effective antiretroviral therapy has decreased the incidence of bacillary angiomatosis.
items.explanation: The incidence of bacillary angiomatosis has decreased since the introduction of effective antiretroviral therapy.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS | Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.statement: Cultures are usually positive in diagnosing bacillary angiomatosis and peliosis.
items.explanation: Cultures are usually negative for bacillary angiomatosis and peliosis.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · PATHOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS | Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS | Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.statement: Blood cultures may be positive in diagnosing bacillary angiomatosis and peliosis.
items.explanation: Blood cultures may indeed be positive when diagnosing bacillary angiomatosis and peliosis.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · PATHOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS | Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Bartonella Infections, Including Cat-Scratch Disease | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.assertion: Clinical presentation of Bartonella infections depends on the infecting species.
items.reason: The immune status of the infected individual also influences clinical presentation.
items.explanation: Both the infecting Bartonella species and the immune status influence clinical presentation, but the reason does not explain the assertion alone.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · Bartonella Infections, Including Cat-Scratch Disease | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS
items.assertion: CSD typically resolves without treatment in immunocompetent individuals.
items.reason: Lifelong immunity is the rule after CSD infection.
items.explanation: In immunocompetent individuals, CSD usually resolves without treatment and lifelong immunity develops.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS
items.assertion: CSD is more common in the southern United States.
items.reason: The incidence of CSD peaks during fall and winter in temperate climates.
items.explanation: While CSD incidence peaks during fall and winter, it's higher in the southern U.S. due to climate factors.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Cat-Scratch Disease
items.assertion: In seronegative CSD patients with severe complications, early biopsy is important.
items.reason: Early biopsy helps establish a specific diagnosis in cases of encephalitis or neuroretinitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why early biopsy is crucial.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · DIAGNOSIS | Cat-Scratch Disease

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS | Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis | ☐ PREVENTION
items.assertion: Prolonged therapy with a macrolide or doxycycline is recommended for bacillary angiomatosis.
items.reason: Bacillary angiomatosis requires antibiotic treatment to manage the infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why prolonged therapy with a macrolide or doxycycline is recommended.
items.reference: Bartonella Infections Including Cat Scratch Disease · PATHOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS | Bacillary Angiomatosis and Peliosis | ☐ PREVENTION
